speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Nellie Wood Smith took place at her home in Bovill on November 3, 1975. The interviewer is Sam Schrager.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: When they made that a law, and voted in that pension business, that was at a time when he'd already been working for him thirty, forty years. And all that time they weren't doing that. All he gets is from that time on until he's retired. The latter part of his labor there was retired, that's all he could draw on. What does it come to for the... Thirty -six dollars and twelve cents.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And how many years is that for?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I don't...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He actually worked for them, you say?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, fifty -three.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: They didn't retire until he was eighty -three years old. Because they couldn't find anybody else that could take the place. He ran a nerve -wrack machine in the machine shop over there. Sometimes half octave, you haven't got a time, it'd go from one to the other. The drill press and the... He had the two lathes there. And then they had the... I don't know, all those things in the machine shop.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So he was indispensable to them?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, he was just...

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: they looked around and they couldn't find anybody that could run all those things. Maybe they could find somebody that could run one of them. But to have somebody take care of all of them, you have to hire five or six men or four, you know, to take care of all those things. Some of them couldn't run all those. He was pretty near born with that idea. He started working in the machine shop in the east when he was twelve years old. He started firing the engine and cleaning up around them. And he just grew up in the machine shop. His older brother was a master mechanic there. And then he was watching him, or whenever time he was putting up some machinery there. Or setting things up in their lathes. He was watching him and he got so he could just learn from his older brother. Just all he needed in the old... So he was quite efficient in the middle of time. And then after he was kind of grown up, the owner of the shop put him out into the foundry. They had a foundry and the connection was there in the machine shop. And he used to be a motor in that foundry. And he'd load up big things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know where he got the idea of coming west from? Where? By Bernie?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: By... He got the idea from the... When his brother and nephew came west. Well, there was another man that had been a Greek friend of theirs. Mr. Hollenbeck. And he had come west and wrote back to them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said, why don't you guys all come out here?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Everything's kind of new and original, and everything's kind of wide open. And lots of things to do, lots of jobs. There's a possibility of lots of work.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And he said, why don't you come on out here?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: He liked it so well. He liked the climate and liked everything about it. And they had been here quite a while. And so I guess they decided to come out and look around and see what it looked like out here. And they liked it. And pretty soon they had hired up and got... ...put in cordwood. So they found that job. The man went from cordwood.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: So they hired up to him. They thought that was just for a meal ticket until they found something different. So I think they let Mark Johlwater. So that man, they're down at Troy there. His name is Swanson, I think. And they boarded right there and lived right there with the Swanson. So they had room for them and all. So that got him started. And then they came on up to Helmer. They got the family out there. They had worked long enough to get money to send back and have the family come. The family moved to Helmer. They lived there for quite a while. There was a little school there. Kids go to school. And then, well, then finally he got a chance to go up toward the Elk River here. It's about the Neenah Hill place. You probably know right along there. It's an old goldenrod mine. And they were working that at the time. So they hired up to that then and worked there for him. And then they got acquainted with a few other people, the same people that we knew. Homesteaders right around that area. And of course we knew Houndick because his wife was staying there with us. And he was working. I guess we had gotten acquainted with them before the others came in the house. And so then he brought them over. We told him to bring the folks over some Friday afternoon or evening or Saturday evening. And they would come over Sunday. Because we knew of these folks. We knew of them and heard so much about them. And then Mr. and Mrs. Houndick knew the whole bunch of them. So it was all a pleasure. Well, they used to come over the next Sunday there. It's been a Sunday for a place. And they came over. We had a nice garden and all kinds of vegetables. We had chickens. We didn't have fried chicken. We'd have fried chicken.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: The main things, we'd have sometimes a big kettle of beans. Which was very decidedly a bachelor's dinner. They could cook beans at home, but ours tasted better some reason or other. But it was a good cook. And so we had those beans and they had lots of nice lettuce. And they had wilted lettuce in there. And they had eggs sliced down over the top of it. And boy, they hadn't cared for lettuce much. But that took their eye, that wilted lettuce. And we had our own big bread. Homemade bread.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I don't know how we'd get an order every once in a while. Whenever we found this one of the neighbors was going out to Helmer. That was their nearest grocery store. And when he'd be going out to Helmer there.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: We'd make out a grocery list. We'd get that in there before the day that he was to go. And he'd bring back ours and everybody else's list in on the whole wagon load. And then you'd go back over to his place that's about a mile to where they live. And that's where he'd leave the stuff. And Miller's. They'd be over there and get ours and take it on home. We had bacon and eggs and everything. We had our own chicken.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So Ernie and his folks really liked being at your place.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yes. They just thought they'd found second heaven for near. We were all Missourians from the same place. Not from the same place, but near. We didn't know them in Missouri, but we were both from Missouri.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: They just got so that the folks we'd only get a kick out of. During the daytime, I'd mention sitting around talking and visiting. The boys would probably get out into the front yard. We had a grassy front yard. And they'd be out there playing some kind of games or something. Or... I don't know how to say it. All kinds of athletics. Athletics, I'm trying to say. And then all things like that. And each fell out trying to outdo the other one. And the stunt's trying to... They'd walk on their hands, you know, all such foolishness, entertainment. They always had such good time. It was a rain show. We could look for them every time. They'd come on over. They knew they were lonesome. Everything out there was mine. And there was another... The Zumstink boys. It was two brothers of them. And they were nice. They had homestead not far from us. And then Ora Hayes had another homestead about a mile and a half up. Another way. So they'd all collect right in that way. We'd have a big dinner. And just as it came, a whole bunch. Sometimes they'd fix it so they'd come and stay all night. They'd fix it. We didn't have a bedroom enough. But they would, in the summertime, sleep out. We had a lot of bedding.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, Nell, I wanted to ask you how Ernie started working for Potlatch.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he start as soon as they came into Bovill?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well... Yes, he... After... After that he... Well, we clicked out there. We moved in town because the folks wanted to give us kids at school. And we had missed one term at school. But when we moved in town... And then... I don't know how that made any difference in it or not. But anyhow, it wasn't long until the group moved into Bovill. It was part of them, anyway. And I think things kind of closed down at the mine or something. But anyhow, they came in and...

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: The Henry Smith was working... Was living near Helmer and then Ernie was out there. And he said he started to move in or to come into Bovill. And of course, he knew we were living here. They moved me out there and he dropped in to see us. And we've got a job right now. They put him to... They had, I think, gotten Henry then to work because he was a master mechanic in the East. And they knew machinery from right to left and upside down. And so... I don't know whether he went up to the shops to see Henry or Paul, but I think Henry's already working there at that time. And they put him in around the machinery anyhow. There's another fellow that had a master mechanic's job. Henry was one of the head workmen anyhow. And he... They put... Then Ernie was running to the summer rather and said, Well, we've got a job for you here if you want it. He was running a donkey engine. So that's the way he started for the potlatch. He was on a donkey engine and I was up in the old Camp 7 way up in here. And he was... He didn't know anything about donkey engines running up to him. But he knew enough about engines and about machinery that he took it right over. And they were...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever see one of them work?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I've never seen him running, but I've seen pictures of him. There's pictures in that book of Ernie's own. Ernie's on one of the engines, I think, in that book. And... So... But they run their cable line way out into the woods and go into a log, a bunch of logs. And then they give the signal, the whistled signal, and that's to clear the track. And then they drag that load in. And then that's... I have to load on to the cars, you know. And that's the way that the logging started that day. And he had gotten them used to that. And then, Sunday, Ernie worked quite a little file at that. And they said he was surprised. He was the best engineer they had, I think, on all their engines at that time. And so when he... When he was still working there at...

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Something happened that they needed to come to the shop. Some of them worked at the shop. And Henry told Ernie, but he said he knew that somebody was going around the shop all right. He worked hard down there, and he had to eat in the East.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And so... He hired then over to these shops and started working there. He was one job at another, whatever, and sent them around here. And finally, he was put right in the shop. At these lathes and all those things. Finally, Henry left and went on to Tico, I think it was. Had a job offer to come up there, and then there's a high school up there that just girls could go to high school.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And... After Henry was gone, there was another man who took the Master Mechanics work. And Ernie was well put in, but it wasn't him. He didn't want to be the boss, but he liked to be good working. He didn't have much education in books and things. He didn't have any of the work, but I think it was in 5th grade or something like that. And he went to school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why did he not want to be a boss?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, he just said these problems come up that could require a book work. Maybe he couldn't handle them, I think mostly. And then responsibility. At all, he didn't want that responsibility. But he liked to do the work. And it got so that after he was put in charge of the... ...lays, they'd send right in from camp. Telephone in, well, the engine never told themselves, gotta have a new brass. Burnout of the General or something like that, you know. And they'd just tell the engine never told themselves. He knew every dimension and he didn't keep it in books either. He just had it up here. And that's the way he always worked. By the time they got in here to get it, they'd run the engine in or send something in for the piece that could be applied out there. Well, he had it turned out on the lathes and the dimensions and they were ready to go. And that's one reason they... It wasn't so easy to replace him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That takes a kind of a real special knack to be able to do something like that. Most people never could. Well, he was one of the...

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, he was one of the traveling men, I think, that used to come in and get their orders for the material for the shops. And Ernie was setting up a piece of work at the time and they kept talking about it and about the work and all. And he asked about... This fellow asked about the way he did it. He just set it up to go from memory and all. And so I said something about the micrometer. And he said, no, you can use one. And he never used one. And the fellow thought it was kind of funny, that fellow doing their lead work without knowing him was a micrometer. That would make it perfect. They got to talking and arguing about it. And then some of the other fellows standing around too, talking about it. And they said, well, I bet you put that up in his way and it would be just as good as your micrometer. So maybe better. Well, now they've got a bet up. They couldn't do it. Ernie, instead of his workman, turned it out and the fellow measured it up and the micrometer was right on the scratch. Still, they never saw that before. You wouldn't have believed it. So that's...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think it was that Ernie had that kind of ability? Is that something just bored with or what?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, kind of. He started doing it when he was probably 12 years old at that and kind of grew up with it. And he didn't have the education enough to figure it out and all the dimensions and all. But he knew them and he'd pick up them and he'd tell you, that's the 32nd or this is the 64th or something like that. And he just knew the tools and he saw them and all. He was used to them for years and I think it was mostly because he didn't have the book training, the schooling, that he really should have had. But he had memorized it. He knew that if he got it, he had to memorize it or else he'd have to start learning again. It's his choice that he memorizes it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So maybe it worked out better for him that he did have it so that he learned things so well.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, where's your shirt?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Hi Ernie.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I got it. I was all right with it.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And that black flag hangs on the bedpost.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yeah,

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: she's going to get you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did I tell you that Axel Anderson, last summer when I was talking to him in Spokane, he told me that Ernie could make anything.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: That all he had to do was just draw him a picture if it was something that he wanted to show him and he could make it just from that. Yeah, he'd go out and pick up a piece of scrap brass or whatever they had to make it out of and take it in and turn it out on the lake. Whatever they needed, whatever they wanted. Let me see.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I don't think for a chance just now, but I have a little trinket and anytime I'd have to be at the shop, I'd have to stand up and turn out some little trinket to another another for me. And there's one thing that was about that big around the money, I didn't end it, it branched out a little bigger at the bottom. There's a candle holder and he turned it out from the other side. A little piece of scrap grass. And then he fixed another one. One time it was about that big around and there was a little bowl, kind of shallow bowl. And then the bottom came out larger. And that was my pin tray just the right size for the pins that I'd rather be sewing. But that disappeared.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this while you were going together?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, that was after I was married.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: After I married, I used to sell.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that the company and other people working realized how good earning was?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yes, because they had a meeting of some of the lepers across there. Well, sometimes business meetings. And the losses told them that they had to, well they couldn't have to lay off some of these little fellas. They were beyond the age, you know. And that they were supposed to be retired. And they were the name of the ones that they, Smith and I don't know, maybe they were the two. One of the folks said, if you're going to lay Smith off, you can keep your mind to. Something like that. And that started the question. And they told him, they said, if you lay Smith off, you can just plan on putting on four or five others to run these different jobs that he can run everyone up. And sometimes two or four at a time. One or the other did adjust, you know. And it's ground off to a certain point where he adjusted to go a little further. And anyhow, he stayed. They didn't let him out. Then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: After he was of retirement age, like 65, did it get a lot harder for him to do the work?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He could just keep on doing it just the same as ever.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yes, and he worked until he was 83. But, oh, he began to slow up a little. It's not much. He'd work all night some nights if he had to have a piece of material, some kind of piece of machinery. And right away, something urgent, he'd maybe fix an engine overnight just to work all night on it. He and maybe somebody else who ever helped.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he do that a lot in the early days?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Mm -hmm.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Stay all night and that kind of thing?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, maybe once in a while.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, sometimes he'd just work several hours after the working time was up. But he'd work for several hours. And then he'd come on home. But once in a while, there's a few times he'd have worked all night. He'd want to be sure and get something out for the urgent. And he'd come maybe get in at home at maybe 3, 4 o 'clock in the morning or something like that. And he'd go and sleep an hour or two and then up and gone again.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you feel about that, that your husband was working so much extra?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, I didn't have much to say about it. I knew that that was what he considered necessary. And it was just urgent stuff. I just expected it every now and then. And they had to see he was two miles up the track up here. That's where the shop was then. And sometimes he'd have to walk those miles. But then sometimes if his fellow that was helping would happen to have a truck and a car, but he'd come home and drive him home. But he took his lunch bill and walked it for years and years, about two miles up there. He liked the hiking, but anyhow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It seems like the company should have given him a lot more.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, they should have raised his wages too. Where he's doing the work of people are mainly a seed. But he should have had their fair, their wages as far as that goes. Or raised his wages quite a bit, but they didn't. But this pension of $36 is just not right for somebody that worked as long as he... Well, I guess that was the rule. It was only from a certain date beyond that time, you see. But they could have raised his wages to compare with it.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: They didn't know about that. But he used to make, well in those days it was considered good wages, but it wouldn't be half as good now.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Everyone's smiling across the snow slip. It's still the end of his checks, you know. With the amounts on $300-$400.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And they tried to get him back on the donkey. Once in a while he'd have to go out on the... To do something, to fix something about the donkey that would have broken down. That's usually something he could fix pretty quick.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So once he got in the shop, he stayed there?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yes, they kept him right in the shop.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So he became their master mechanic?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, the master mechanic would have had the whole lead say -so, the boss of, you know. He'd work with him, but he didn't have the responsibility of the whole thing. Was a very good boss. He'd do all the work that he wanted to, and then some. He didn't like being the boss.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was beautiful in 1914.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: What...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did it develop? How did the knowledge of the fire reach town?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Oh, it didn't take a lot to reach town. You look out and you see a smoke rolling up, and that may be four, five, ten miles from here. But the void rolled up, and that smoke rolled up, and you'd know pretty soon there was a fire.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: So at that time, that fire came in to us from out across northwest of us here, up Camp 7 -Lane. And they had firefighters out, but you got away from them, you see.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And when the mother and them went up to see what the other end of town, so they could see better, and see how that looked. And they came back and said, boys, I know the mother seemed to be kind of astonished there. She had never seen English quite like that before. And she said, oh, just a boiling up, smoke boiling up above the trees, you know, those tall trees. And the tall timber. And, well, and green stuff, you know, too. And then there's young stuff. But anyhow, she said that there's this big old heavy smoke and black smoke and all, and just clouds of it. And finally it looked like some of it was just like a big ball of gas. It would roll in, you know, and it would dip down near the ground, and it touched some of the weeds, and touched some of the underbrush and the little timber, you know, and the grass growing. Or some of the limbs, you know, come loose from the fire and then drip down and catch. And when it catches in that dry grass, it's just like an explosion, and it's just the whole thing is going. It crosses right across the middle of that grass and all things like that, you know. Those things are treacherous.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how did, is that how your mother found out about it, just from seeing it? She could see it coming, or did somebody come and tell?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, we knew it was a fire there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was out of the way, so that she thought, well, just how bad was it? How good was it if it was creeping in toward town or anything?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And went up there, and it was, and they were just coming, getting pretty close. And so they hustled on back then, and she said, you know, the people up on the other end of town are getting ready to leave. They're all getting ready to leave town. And we thought it was kind of funny, because that kind of stuff is news to us. And Lisa turned around and talked about it a little bit, and I said, well, maybe we'd be wise if we went in there and gathered up a few things. If they're getting by there, she said, the women up there on the other end of town are digging up the ground and burying their silverware and their dishes and things like that. And she was talking about all of them. She'd heard them talk about it. So when I came down, I said, well, maybe it'd be a good idea if we went in there and gathered up a few of our things and maybe put them in those old trunks that we had there. Each one of us had our trunks in this big old -fashioned, you know. And I said, there's nothing in there but junk. It's just all knick-knacks and stuff. It had no value. It just keepsake, you know. So she said, maybe it would be. It might be a good idea. And we said, if we had to leave town, we'd have a little bit of stuff to take. And if we didn't, why, we'd unpack it again, I guess. We didn't have time for any of that, but we thought we did. And so we were in there unpacking. Oh, these are pretty boxes, candy boxes. We'd come in to a big box of candy every once in a while or something, you know, novel or something pretty.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: So we had been getting out all the boxes and all the trinkets. So we'd have room in the trunk. And I had to get mine just about half unloaded. And Mother was unloading hers and the other girl with her. I guess next door to mine. And my sister had sent them, but she looked out the side door and she said, well, there comes Ernie.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And so we thought, well, he's coming down here for something about the fire maybe. And we went ahead unpacking because we began to think it must be more serious than we thought. So we waited, but we were still working at it. And I said, well, I thought you said Ernie was coming. And she said, well, he was. He was right up the street up here a little ways and he was coming on the run. And so I stepped through into the dining room from the kitchen where I was and looked. The doors were all in line. Looked through that way. And he was in the front room and just going this way with the clothes, taking them down. And they are good clothes and suits and things, you know. He was getting the clothes out of the clothes closet. It was the closet beside the door. And so I said, well, he's in there. I said, well, he's in the front room there. He's getting their clothes and things. So I went back and went to hurry and the thieves a little faster. And they came right along and just dumped them all in and they'd like to dump me in there with them. I was all stooped over there taking out those things, you know, unpacking. And he just threw them all in, good clothes and all. And I had to fight that way out from under him. I said, well, he just shipped me along with him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So he was a lot worse than you thought. Yeah. You were taking your time getting the stuff out to repackage. Yeah, they were going to do our things nice and then there wouldn't be anything upset or anything.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: We'd just start from where we left off and unpack if we didn't need to go by it. If it was something to do, unpack and we can build the difference. It wouldn't be messed up. But anyhow, we got those... And he said, you know, then, well, he said they did all that they could at the camp at the machine shops and all because they'd wet everything down with a hose. They just soaked everything there because the fire was getting so close there to them. And there's people that lived up there. And he and another fellow had soaked all the buildings, you know, all the roofs and everything so that they wouldn't catch very easy. And so they did all they could so then they struck out for town to see what would become the rest of us. And they... I think, yeah, he said, I think that he came down ahead of that... No, he didn't. He came after it. That engine that was bringing the people from the camp that was just this side of the machine shops a little ways is what they call slab town. And that's where the several families lived there. Almost eight or ten people, I guess, families that lived there. And so they had stopped and he said when he came in that he said the people up there in the town are getting... They come out and they're pulling out or they're leaving and shipping them out or something like that. And so they had come down already on the freight train, on the flat cars. And he said that they couldn't do any more up there so he moaned down here and told us that when we began to hurry. And then he said, come on, get yourselves ready here and let's go to... So we can get down there. They're waiting down at the depot to carry the people out here, to take the people out on the flats.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And he said to Dad, he said, Dad, don't you go get the dray and have them pick up these trunks, will you?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, Dad struck out there on high and there was an old fellow had a dray line here. Saint Germain was his name.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Pretty soon he came back with the dray man and he backed right up to the back door and what mean of how, we saw him come, you see.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And he said, what do we do?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, the trunks were just old and they had no locks or keys or anything. And there was always so much plundering going on at times like that. Somebody, there was always a lot of snooping into everything. And we wondered what we'd do about that. And there was no way of tying it. They had no ropes or anything. Ernie said, no, we couldn't fix it because we didn't have the rope. I said, well, go get the clothesline. And I said, well, he couldn't. He didn't have time to cut it. I said, well, take the axe and cut it down. And so he did and he whistled out and got it and had his clothesline all wound up and put him in the dray with our trunks. And he shoved him right in there with them to tie, no, dad shoved him in there to tie those ropes, those trunks. Now, after he got on the dray and on the flat cars up there. So, when he got there just in time up the depot with our trunks, just to load right off the wagon and into the flat cars. Just to boost around flat cars. And dad with them. Because we told him, we all told him, don't you dare leave those trunks a minute. You get them all tied down. You take care of those because that may be all they'll ever have. Because they're burning out. So, dad thought he had to do that. He knew he'd all be on his neck.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You went down to the, you walked down to the depot yourselves?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: We did, but the flat cars, dad pulled out them by the time. Dad just got there in time to get those on there and the train pulled out. And little dad went on down as far as Derry and I guess he got the things marred up at that time. He had them put off at Derry.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And he got them marred sometime either on this way or after he got there. And then he got somebody to bring him back up here so he wasn't became a mother.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What became of you? You went down to the depot and you said you were dressed up in a lot of clothes?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I was really dressed up. Well, as soon as, after we got started out with our trucks, well then we began to think that they were hurting us. Well, we better get on there then. Before that train pulled out, we didn't know. But instead of that, it was gone when we got there. But they had run in some passenger coaches from Potlatch. At that time, they had an engine come up here and bring up some passenger coaches to take people out of here. And so we all got through the start. I was expecting to be saying I was expecting my baby any day then. Because the doctor told me that I can expect him around the 5th of August. He thought I don't take any notes because I was planning on being... I don't know. Maybe I'm getting that crooked. I planned on the 5th of August because he told me one time there, checking me beforehand, if you go beyond the 25th of July, you'll probably go to the 25th of August. I don't think he knows what he's talking about but I would argue. But anyhow, that was the 3rd of August when this fire came along. So I thought, well, anything could happen, I guess. So I must go prepared. So I got my suitcase that I had everything packed in that I had laid away for a whole month in readiness. The thing happened and all I had to do was pick up the suitcase and it was all there. I had a couple sheets and a couple diapers and a little outfit. Just the first dressing intact there. I picked up my suitcase and then they'd taken all the blankets and things off of the beds, most of it, and spread them out on the floor and dumped all the pillows and covers in them and tied four corners. They went with the chunks. So we'd have some bedding.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: When I started to go down there, I happened to think, oh, well, I had my dress. I had my aunt's dress there, my great -aunt.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I was so big that I couldn't wear any decent clothes. But Aunt Mary, I borrowed one of her dresses one time because we all wanted to go somewhere. She weighed about 225 or 250. I had borrowed her dress so I could wear it at whatever I was going to do. I still had it there. I hadn't sent it home yet. I had my old mother Hubbard, just plain blue calico dress on. It was the only thing I could wear. I happened to think as I was about to pass the little clothes closet in my bedroom, there was Aunt Mary's dress hanging there. I said, oh, my goodness, I can't let that dress burn up. I grabbed it and put it on over top of what I had. It was nice material. It was a good dress. It had a lacy front in there. I gave a nickel -fat picture of some of that stuff. It was a bit funny at the time, but it got to be funny afterwards. I put the dress on and I had that safe, I figured. Then I started to go out there and I said, oh, my goodness, I can't go like this. There's only two diapers in that suitcase. If anything had happened to me along the way, that's the first thing I'd have to do, send somebody to the store down there for an outing flannel.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: For what?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: For outing flannel, for baby flannel, to make diapers if anything like that should happen. I said, they'd have to go to Potlatch store there and get some. I waited a minute. I ran and I had another blue calico dress hanging there. I wasn't taking that, but finally I wondered what in the world can I take them in. I saw that dress and I spread it on the bed. Then I piled in a few dozen, all the diapers I could get. I had, I think, five or six dozen made and stored in a little dresser. They couldn't come over there by me. I piled them into that and tried four corners of that blue calico, big dress. Then I could run my arms through them, the corners tight. I had that all right. I went to the front room then and I was getting ready to go outside there on my guitar. No, I think it was the hat I saw first. But anyhow, there was my hat. I had a white horsehair braid hat. It was quite a pretty thing. They had just been here. They used to come in every week or two, or once a month anyhow, with millinery goods. There were sewing hats and things. I had bought a beautiful blue. It's a Delft blue, I guess you'd call it. It was more like a, it was kind of a variegated slightly, but it was an ostrich plume. It was, I think, quite as far as they spliced those things on the feathers, you know, to make them droopy, make them a lot longer. I had bought a plume for that hat just about before. And I just couldn't stand to see that hat go up in the smoke. I grabbed it and put it on. My hair was, the hair was the worst mess it is now. All over the place, I hadn't combed it yet. I'd been busy packing. So I pinned it on right through.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: On top of your other hat?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: No, I didn't have any hat on. I just had my hair all stringed around. I went to string your hair. And then I happened to think about, took a look behind the curtain just right beside the door there. And oh, there was my guitar. And I've had that ever since I was 12 years old. Mother and Dad got it for me for Christmas. And I just couldn't stand to see that go up in the smoke. So I loaded that on. And I can't remember what I looked like, but here I was. A suitcase in one hand and the big bun of diapers in the other hand.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: The guitar tucked under this way. And the hat, I don't know whether I had anything more. But that's about all I could carry. And I was just going along there, wobbling.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And so, we all started out then to walk to the hippo.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: So anyhow, we got out the front of our lot where we joined the street. And Aunt Mary and her husband and her brother were coming from there. She was my aunt, great aunt. They had just come up the street and joined us in front of our house there. And Aunt Mary had dressed us a little bit the same way, but she had things gathered up. And she thought that they had mostly suitcases on. And she had to see, she had kind of a Mexican sun hat. She always wore one when she was out running up and down to our place or any, you know, just shade for shade. So she had that hat on while she was working around. And she happened to look up hanging on the nail. And the micro -divers, that was her good hat. And she didn't want to lose that. So she grabbed it and put on that peak and put a pin through it on the top of that peaked hat.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of hat was this you put on? On top of it?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, that was a dress hat, really. A Sunday good meeting dressy hat. And it was pinned onto the top of that straw hat, sun hat. She looked funny with that dang lip on the top of that and a pin run through it to hold it.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where you going?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I guess he decided he wants a different chair for me. For some reason or other. And... And then she had some suitcases and traveling bags. And Uncle Ed, her husband, had some of them. And her brother, Uncle Henry Smith, had... Hair. Now, I think I heard you spitting around there. No, that's... Don't go. There's your spit cans over there.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And... Uncle Henry was going on, he had two of these men's traveling bags, you know, that I used to have. And he had them in his hands and he was very big, about 250, big tummy on him. And he had them and he walked with a wobble. He walked on, he was dressed in... Well, he was always kind of dressed up and wasn't working or anything. And he had his white shirt and suit on. And he chewed the back on. And he was talking on the individual end. I pew -tew you, he says. He spit and it all splattered on his shirt.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: He said, well, I'm so dumb scared I can't stay over there, I'll spit over my chin.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, he just left.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, he kept on and got down to the depot. Got on the train then. And then they just had time to kill there for about five hours before the train was... They were just waiting for the emergency signal to... Any time that they thought was necessary, they'd give a signal and then pull out that train and thought it was on them. But they kept us there until about ten o 'clock, I think it was. We were thinking it went down about five. And it's about ten o 'clock that they came in and notified us that... They said, folks, just as well go home now and go to bed and get some rest. Because they think that the danger's over for the night. The wind had changed and was blowing it kind of back away from us.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So we said, you want to get some rest?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And then he said, if it does get any worse, give a signal so that you can all get back in time. And there'd be four long whistles of that engine. I didn't know that. And that 23 was the engine that pulled the things. It had the main logon engines. And anyhow, it had a terrible siren of a whistle on it. And it said we'd give four long signals. And that means, come on, that there's danger. And so we all went home and there wasn't much place to sleep and much bedding or anything. But we got by. And anyhow, when we were asleep in bed, it was about three o 'clock in the morning. But I heard the whistles start. He had to wake me up. I was so tired, I guess, from what he'd run home from the shop and been working that he slept through it. I heard that whistle start blowing. And I laid real still. Here come another one. I laid still. And I thought, oh, I'm afraid that's it. And there came the third blast. And I thought, no, I was just kind of waiting. And here comes the fourth blast. And I not to turn in. I said, hey, there's your whistle.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And he said, what was that?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I said, well, that was four long whistles from that engine. Boy, he just jumped out of the bed into his bitches and they kind of run. And he said, well, if folks get ready, I'll run up and see if it's okay. I'll see what the alarm's about. He said, you know, he'd better go and find out. And the smoke was so thick in town, you could hardly get your breath. It just got in your eyes, you know, just real smoky. The whole town.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: So we were up, getting ready to go. We didn't do much undressing or fussing around. He said, he come back then, I'm running on back. He said, no, it's a false alarm. We wouldn't have to go. He said, there's some kind of a mix -up in the instructions. And they blew that blast of four long, shrill whistles, you know. And that was to call the night crew, firefighting crew, call them in and send the day crew out. So, that was, they kind of mixed up their instructions. So we didn't have to go down. But while we were down there on that train, I was standing around looking at everything that was going on. And everybody else was too. Well, my sister and I lived up there at the cafe at Slabtown. And, but you can fall down. You better be careful.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: You better be careful. You'll fall down here. Okay. You want this cane? Hey?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You want this? You want the cane, Ernie?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, you'd be a little more sure of yourself if you'd take it. But his sister, my sister -in -law, lived up there at Slabtown. And they'd been working hard all day. They'd been Ernie, she and their daughter both. The daughter was, I guess, about 12, 13 years old. And they'd been ironing and just working like beavers. Had a big bush and she'd run from the house down the, kind of down the hill. They had a pig pen down there where they carried some water down to the pigs and the chickens and all. And she gave them a good cool, awful hot. And it was, of course, in August.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And they, they, do you want to try that?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: They got on there, they had to change clothes because they were, they had on their regularly dirty clothes. And they wanted to hurry and get that ironing done so they could have a good bath with them and clean up. And they were just making the best of what they had then. And that caught them at that time. And she'd come in with a dirty dress on and she was from Finicky with her housekeeping, her clothes and everything. She was called Nasty Nice, she might, we always call them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What does that mean, Nasty Nice?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, just extra nice and that's fanciful. Just too nice for any good use. And so she got on there to the car and the little girl,

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: the girl went to, had the scrubbing and the combing her hair. And her mother was fixing her up and then washing herself and then she had her own hair to comb and hand out some clothes from the suitcases and get dressed. But, and her stockings, well she'd kicked off her shoes because her feet were bothering with standing and ironing and all. And she was running around in stockings, she had some old stockings on anyway. And she's running around in her stockings feet and well, up and down that little hill, oh it must have been the legs of the loft probably from the house or maybe a little more. And they're just kind of through the brush and she's snagged until the stockings were, well the heels of them were clear up. Like those things. Around her ankles. Yes, the back of them were clear up and they were raggedy. So, I know it's just a picture to see, a picture from life's other side. And to see her as she was and the girl and alongside of her were some of the ladies that lived in the edge of town here. The banker's wife and the hotel lady, the hotel and I don't know, several people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: T .P.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Jones is what?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yes, well I guess, I don't know if she was here at the time or not. She might have been gone but I don't remember her in particular. But Mrs. Nelson as the banker's wife and Mrs. Ellison and some of them up in that corner that, well kind of, I guess kind of thought they lived on Bug Row or something, you know, or on the kind of fancy dressers and for snickety. And here they were. Those two women that I mentioned, Nelson and Ellison, they had, they had, did like we did. They were going to take their good things, you know, and have them saved. I guess they buried their linens and their silverware and everything. I thought they must have got advanced, how they got their advanced notice of all that I just couldn't see. When we didn't know anything about it, but of course they had a draft there where they could see it from here above it too. But they had gotten all dressed up in their best clothes and all. And they put on their things, you know, to take them all. And then, here's in August, but one of them had her furs on and her muff, you know, just as you can imagine.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And their hats and just like they were going to a wedding or something somewhere.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: It must have really been a sight. It was. And the little variation, each little bunch was still gathered. It had something to do with the time. And then the time came and everybody was hungry and the stores had burned out just a day or two before. No, that was, they'd been, they'd burned out the third of July. And, well, they had set up a tent and that kind of place had a little town park thing up there. That was just right close to the depot. They had set up tents and they were trying to have a store there to keep all necessities and keep people going until they could do a little better. And, well, people would run in and get over there and get some bread and whatever they could get, they didn't have to cook. And sausages or any kind of cheese or any lunch meats or anything like that. And then our bunch went over and got some things and maybe cookies if we could eat us a little snack.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And the doctor then would come by the cars and drop in up there, and, you know, they said, how you coming? How you getting along?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I said, fine. I'm having lots of fun. So, well, just hang on here and take it easy. And if the worst comes to worst, we'll just turn one of these coaches into the hospital and we'll have that baby right here. But on the way to potlatch if necessary. He said, don't let it bother you any. I wasn't. I was just taking it as a come. I couldn't help but laugh at part of things. You could laugh at it even then. Yes, and my laugh at it myself partly. But such a combination of outfits that I had on. And like I said, remind me of the pictures I've seen of the big old Irish washerwoman or the Negro washerwoman. You see them with the big bundle of clothes on their head and big ones that they're carrying in Fort Cornish time. So it reminds me of those pictures I've seen.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, were the people down there at the waiting at the depot, do you think they were more fearful or more hopeful or were they both?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I guess both.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: They all stayed right there because they expected the engine to pull out in a minute maybe. They were waiting until the people all got there and then they figured they better not go back because they might have to run right back in a few minutes. Because they were just standing guard and watching that fire. And then at, I think it was 10 o 'clock, the wind changed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you very afraid about losing the house and everything you had?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, we were very perturbed about it. And yet we didn't have time to go. We said just take it or leave it. We figured if it's there we'll be glad to have it. But if it's gone, there's nothing to do about it. And we kind of had to take that attitude because they couldn't tell whether the whole town might burn out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the mood like on the train? What were people talking about when they were waiting all those hours?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, just a little bit of everything. And someone said about what I did and what somebody else did and all that. That the awful things and how they tried to gather up a few things. And one fella from Camp 8 up the settlement there at Camp 8 right across the track from the shops. He was a timekeeper I think. But anyway, he had the... They were going to go get on those flat cars and let them out. And he grabbed up... I think it was a puppy that they had a kitten. No, I think it was a puppy. They grabbed up the pup in his arms and grabbed the mop. And he had that... they all laughed about that. He had the mop and that little pup I think was all he had with him. And his wife was still trying to gather up some things and all. And he ran on to get on the train and they told it on him. They said that he got way over the train and left her way long behind.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And she was getting up things that were worthwhile and all. But he was so excited. That's all he got.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They said, what in the world here is the thought of saving that mop?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And then, you see, they had to come from Camp 8 up there down to the... They had the track run right through Slabtown on the way to town. Well, when they got down to Slabtown, they picked up all these other people that had been packing and... Well, everything they had burned all those people. The fire got them and the fire didn't get the shop, so they wet it down thoroughly. And that helped it from sparks and all. And I don't know why, but to be able to change the wind or something, it might have made a difference. But anyhow, the shops didn't go, but just on the way down the track, there was Slabtown. And all the buildings burned there. The fish and everything they had burned.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And they got on these flat cars. Got the people on there and what stuff they gathered up, put it on. But it was so smoky. And all they couldn't hardly get their breath on those cars. And they had to take blankets and put over their heads to breathe. Hardly get to them. Blanketed their heads to look at the town. And then about halfway between the Slabtown and Bovill, the fireman read somebody. They called him Red, you know. He looked out the engine, looked out the window there on the engine. And he's the fireman of the engine. And he looked back and said, hurry up. He said, Tarbox, get ready to jump. There's a tree across the track, something like that. Get ready to jump. And, come on, hurry up or something like that. And Tarbox just grabbed the wrench and turned around and turned on him. And he said, you jumping out of this crony with this wrench, you shovel coal faster than you ever shoveled in your life. And you pour it on. And the kid kind of got scared of him because he was drawn back to whacking one. So he went to pouring the coal on. And by the time they got down to that thing, he had her good and hot. And there was a tree had burned and fallen across the track. And right in front of that train load of people. And so Tarbox said, the only thing he could do was fire it up and hit it hard. Maybe he could break it. And there was a, looked like an average, you know, a small enough tree that he thought he could break it and go on through. And he hit that, and he broke the thing, broke the tree and went on through with his screw. Got there, everything safe and sound.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did that train have the men coming down from the camps as well as the people from Slabtown?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, I don't know whether there was many of the workmen from some of the camps. I don't, I think they were already out, maybe, from that one camp up in that area. And they were fighting fire anyway. All the men they could get were fighting fire. And so they, I guess, had different ways, I don't remember about that. I guess they had different ways of taking care of them and getting them in and out as they could. And then Tarbox was the one that run that engine, you know, that had to break that and come in. He was the one that later on, when they were going to, they had, they had...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, you think you had the armistice?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yeah, the armistice. You told me that from the same guy. Same one that was...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was, I had heard that T .P. Jones had said, let it burn.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that true?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I don't know. Somebody told me that was the beginning when it first started, the fire first started.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, they, sometimes if they, you know, they, if they don't see any chance of saving the thing, and it'll endanger the men and all, and kind of, sometimes they get surrounded and locked in, you know. Makes it pretty tough.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, was the smoke bad in town there during the afternoon, that afternoon when you went down to the... No, it wasn't bad.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was just at night that it got bad, huh?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The air settled, you know.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I guess the dampness, the hot fire, I guess, but that's, we always have an evening dew here.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: At night, you're reading the graphs, right?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: It's just like reading a trick.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: It's just, I guess they have that down where you live, don't they?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It's worse in the canyon than it is on top of the ridge.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, then, then, when the next morning came, was the danger gone?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Mm -hmm.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: It was, but for goodness sakes, we kept the doors shut as tight as we could, because until it helped the smoke get kind of clear, you know, kind of get blown away or lifted or something, and it helped it because if you had your door open by that smoke, just come right in. And it made it awful. Tough.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Thankfully, we had a house to go to. Ours wasn't that much about it. Ours was just like the pictures over on the... No, it doesn't show the house all that.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: But, oh, it's a picture that's in that book that shows us stopping from the house, my family. And from the house that was just out there. And it was just a small place that they had built the houses, you know, to take care of us. When we moved out there, we just hurried up and threw that up there in a hurry to have a place to live until we could do better. Until we could find a place to buy or build or something. And we bought that lot and built a little house on it.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I just...we had plenty of room for us.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Fire just sounds incredible on the whole experience.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yeah, nothing to be sneezed at. Even though some of it was pretty funny after we was all over with. It must have been fun that day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I suppose when you went back home in the evening, you must have felt better about it.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yeah, I mean, we thought, oh dear, what a mess. But I'm thankful to have it. It was a mess. Stirring everything up, you know, crap here and there. And all kinds of things mixed together in what we did take in those chunks. I think I went and grabbed the silverware and stuck it in there. And everything we think of in a minute, you know, you can't think of things. And I told my mother when I said, now let's go and unpack those chunks. And put some things, you know, more valuable, things that can mount to something. Or we'll probably get there with something like the fellow, you know, the drum that...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What, just toss the little glass out the window from upstairs and carry the feather bed down or something? You know?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Pillows. I said they'd probably wind up with a lot of stuff in there, grabbing it in a hurry that didn't mount to the halo beans. And they just stuff it, they couldn't re -buy it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But then Ernie rushed you out anyway?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yes. We had some lunch and went over to the tent store.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Got by alright.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: But, you know, that other fire that they had from up around Dent... I don't know.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: You were dead up in South, beyond Southwick or something up in there. Well, that was more or less wilderness at the time. It's kind of all open country now up there. But there were some people that we knew real well, Wriste. Mr. Wriste. And they had a son and daughter. They were just little kids. And they were homesteading up in there. And they buried their stuff and a lot of it when they... And they just got out with the skin of their teeth. They were living up there. I don't know whether they burned their place or not. But somebody told me just recently that somebody from up there said yes. And they never had found where they buried some stuff. They never could find it again, I think.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I think that was the Bowles. Because I think I mentioned that to you when I was here before. Because Wallace Bowles told me that they never found their silverware. That was the same time, about 1910.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Didn't that spread from around this way and wind up at St.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Mary's?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And it burned a lot of men. It burned up some men then. I don't remember the sample. We were burned up in that. Quite a few.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was really bad around Avery and Wallace.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the fire of this one in 1914, was it the wind that stopped it? Was that it?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, it kind of blew back and changed it.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, some people had different ideas. Of course, that was a big change. John says that God had something to do with that. He changed the wind.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: The way of the wind. The direction. I can't say it, but I had a light stroke several years ago. Ever since that, I can't think of a word that I want. Sometimes it's just a little old word. They didn't bother me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You don't seem to have any trouble to me.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, I guess I'm such a yabble that I talk a lot. I don't know what I say so much. I get mixed up sometimes trying to think of a word. Simple thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this John Sanderson?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: He said that fire was coming right for their house. It was just getting right close. I don't know. He told about how far, but I don't remember what he said. He said it was bound to just come in a pair and heading right for their place. He said he just grabbed his wife and said, Come on, we've got to go.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: They prayed to God that they would save the house. That things would be spared and that things would be all right. He said almost immediately that the wind turned and the fire began going the other way. I can say that there's a lot of truth in things like that.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Such things like that have happened to good wind plants. You can think back to things that God had a hand in that men couldn't handle. He said that when it stopped right there, they would tell him that God didn't answer prayer.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did John Sanderson think that God caused the fire not to come into Bovill itself?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I don't know whether he... I never heard him say anything about that. He said he knew that they stopped at their place just like a wall of the fire.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: He said that that would save their place there.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: No doubt there's several of them that might have been doing a lot of praying about that. They can maybe help to save the town as far as that goes. There's no secret to what God can do. That's the song. What He's done for others, He'll do for you. Those are my two sons who are part of the earth. They're in the sea, God can do you.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, He did a lot for all of us at that time, I figure. We all feel pretty lucky that He doesn't know what God is. God has handed a lot of things that He never gets credit for.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think at the time, now when that happened, after it was over, did most people feel that it was luck? Or did most people feel that it was something religious going on?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, I can't say. I don't know how they all felt. But, of course, anything like that, it was generally, oh well, it didn't happen anyhow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It didn't like that, did it?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: They didn't give credit to cross that cat on the side of that porch. His color shows up plain, we can step. You've got good eyesight. At a distance, I can see that I'm being nearsighted as can be. And I can hardly read the fine print in the newspaper anymore. I can, if I've rested a little bit, I don't take much determine. I have an appointment as an eye doctor for the 27th.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I think I should probably get going to get lunch. Well, I don't, it'll be there lunch for quite a while. It's generally one or two o 'clock, something like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I wanted to ask you about the Jones Institute. And I had heard that Mrs.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: T .P.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Jones was sort of the town's leading socialite while T .P. was working for Potlatch.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that true? Did she sort of run the town in those years?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: She did, try to, anyway.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And you've been talking to somebody that was an old timer here. And what they said was no job was true, but because she, well, I guess it's not traffic talk about people who have passed away. But it's true. She did, I don't know whether, why she was that way. But she did, well, she got a hold on things and she always, you know, his name was Tom Jones. And so she always called him Tommy, Tommy and Al. And when they'd be talking about things, when they turned to collecting money for some of it, or, well, she was a great hand to get in on the Red Cross at that time. And she was, well, she was the boss, made herself the boss of it. And she'd go out in the camps, you know, and collect from the men for the Red Cross businesses. I don't know what all they did with it, or what she told them to do with it, but they didn't have as far Red Cross. And she'd collect herself from the men and one of them would say, well, I don't have any money. I don't have any money now. Oh, that's all right. You just give me permission and I'll have Tommy take it out of your check. So, I don't have any money with me now. I could give it to you later maybe. I don't know, I'll have Tommy take it out of your check. I'll be all right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She was pushy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, was she?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And she would get another five dollars from each one. She had the cinch on her, if they said no, she'd lose her job. So, she had them in the corner.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Mrs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Jones was, how did the social life go at that time?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She was at the center of it.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: She didn't mix too well with the common people. She kind of, well, she was bossy on some things, but some occasions and some events that were taking place, she didn't always get in on. She kind of stayed to herself in a way, but pretty much everything she could get in on. She was about to be boss of us. Well, I was at the time of the, I was president of the cemetery committee up here, the cemetery. And we were trying to get that cemetery in the working order. It was just a big weed patch and it was a disgraceful looking place. And so we decided, there's a bunch of us women, decided we'd go up and do something about it. They didn't get the men at it and they were all working anyhow. So, we thought, well, we'd go up and dig around a little and pull weeds and we could make an effort. So, we started in and I was president at that time. And so we thought, well, we'd gone as far as we could go. And we had, there was stumps there, but they'd taken the big trees, you know, and they'd been cut there. And there'd been, oh, I don't know, there's humps and holes. Sometimes we'd be blasted out a tree.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And so we decided that we'd get busy and make some money some way or other and hire somebody to do that to do it.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: So, we talked it over and we decided we'd just keep going until we got that, fill up some of those holes and plow it out and have it plowed and harrowed, you know, right?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And we'd do it and we'd plow it out with grass. That was all our plans. Well, we decided to give a big oyster supper at the restaurant. And so we rented the balls of dining room for the evening and we had the big oyster supper there. And we took in $163 .03.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And that is, well, not that on the, I didn't take that all in on the supper. That was part. But that particular time, we had great big tents. It was when the, what do you call this, armistice was signed and we had a big play on the, big to -do on that. And there was great big tents, enormous tents set up all over that little park up there in the town. And well, we had all kinds of entertainment and everything and singing and music and everything. And we served a big dinner. We had gotten, that time was a big dinner and had, I guess, I guess the oyster stew that we had, I think, came before that. We had, but it didn't make enough, it made good that we didn't have enough to do what we wanted to do. And so we were in on that and she came just as nice as pie. But Mrs. Smith now, we were just thinking, the Red Cross had so many things gathered up for them. And we thought it'd be kind of nice if we'd like to give you ladies a chance at that, if you would like to have the chance of serving dinner, if you wanted to have a big dinner down there. And we considered it and we thought it would be a good idea. And that's where we served dinner, to lumberjacks. Well, there's a whole, I don't know, supper, I guess. I don't think we had it in the early day, but in the early part of the day, I mean. But it was, I guess, our supper because we lasted up into the night and all the entertainment. And well, we had everything. Oh, we had big roasts and all the goodies and wonderful dinner there. And all the lumberjacks and everybody came in there to have their dinner and they raved about it. So, some of the ladies waited tables, so we had them all little tables all through there, you know, and everything fixed just so soon. Had our hot dishes we'd carry it over to serve it hot. And my sister -in -law had been sick in bed. She used to live right next door here. She'd been sick in bed for several days, but she was one of us. She thought, well, she'd try and get up and make it down there. She'd try and help, so she felt a little better. So she went down to go. She got up, went to the party down there and I said, I said, Lou, I said, you can sit. I said, why don't you just sit and take, leave the cash here. I said, take the cash and that'll be easier on you. And she said, yes, you thought you could do that. And that's the way it was arranged. So we went along into the evening, went pretty well into the evening until we were getting, oh, I don't care the finish of it. Mrs. Jones was there and of course she was busy, busy, busy like an old bumblebee. And she finally said, she said, well, Mrs. Smith, couldn't you take, you're such a good cook and so handy at these things. Well, couldn't you take this dish up, put her in the house and dishing up and serving these meals and let Mrs. Camel take it. Well, Mrs. Camel was her sister -in -law and let her take the cash and exchange for that. And she could have done it, it wouldn't have hurt her. She was a little thing, but it wouldn't hurt her. But she changed them around and when the thing was over, Mrs. Camel got up and took the money and put it off up to her house. That's the last we saw of it for a good long while. Mrs. Jones is doing this now.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: That's when they had the $163 .03.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's what you made from that?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That one thing. For the cemetery, not for the Red Cross. No, it's for a cemetery.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: We then asked about how much we made in all of it. I didn't know it just yet because there's some things we bought. We'll have to pay for the ice cream and some of the things and all this and that. It couldn't sell back until we got it open for her. I'm not too mad yet, but think about it. We'd wait a long while and ask about it again. Well, she hadn't had a chance to sell those yet. And she had put this off and put this off. And found that the ice cream had come from St. Mary's Creamy up there. She hadn't got around to do that yet. I guess it was months before we ever got any settlement out of her. She had been working for the young folks and they had adopted the care of an orphan who received. And they had to pay for her first. And then we would have others left. I don't know what all she didn't have, but anyhow, they'd have to go every once in a while. I felt that was just all on my shoulders. I was Secretary of the President of the thing. I didn't know how to get it settled out of her. Anyhow, she said, she finally said, well, we'd waited until it last there. I met her on the street down there right where that corner cafe is now, or that tavern. It was the bank at that time. And then that service station was right out in the middle of the street there. I met her and I had asked her so many times, I asked her to come and meet with us, or have us meet with her. But we didn't want to go to her house because there was one lady that had gone to her house. It was the Red Cross lady who was supposed to have the... Well, she was the Secretary of the Red Cross lady, but Mrs. Jones had collected the money and she had it. The other woman, the Treasury, the Treasurer, couldn't get it from her. So we were not taking any chances. Oh yes, Mrs. Whitty was the one who was the Treasurer and she was the dentist's wife here. She went up to Mrs. Jones one day and was asking about it and tried to get the Red Cross money. But she was responsible for that Red Cross money because she had to hold that office and nobody else was having the running of it, the spending of it or anything. I couldn't blame her for not wanting that straightened out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She couldn't get it though?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: She couldn't get it and she finally got kind of mad at her. Mrs. Jones said, come on tell me, throw her out. Throw her out. I said, put her out of here. Mrs. Whitty said, don't bother yourself. When I get ready, I'll go out. I won't have to be thrown out. I'll leave on my own accord when I get through. And she did too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you met her on the street?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I got out in the middle of the street and she didn't even feel me anywhere. But I asked her then, I said, now we're having a meeting. I don't know if it was that afternoon or the next day or something, again of these ladies. And I said, they're all anxious to know what we're going to do about this. We're needing our money to go ahead and fix up the work up there. We had already hired some men to do some work and we owed them for that. I told her about it and I said, we'd like to get this settled. I said, we've asked her so many times to meet with us and settle it somewhere. We've never gotten anything done. I said, we'd like that settlement. Well, she said, I told her, we've already hired some men to plow and harrow up there and all. She said, well, they didn't need it or something like that. I said, well, no, I said, that was surveyed in the first place. I said, there's a great deep hole there where they blasted out a stump. Maybe the peg of that survey is right in the bottom of that hole.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I said, what are you going to do? Let somebody stand and hold that peg while they filled it up and put it down again?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Oh, that's not so. She knew full well that that wasn't true because Tommy and I go up there on our corner lots, the corners of our lot, and look at it every once in a while and never got it out. She said, she knew very well. I said, then I walked right up to her and I looked at her and I said, Mrs. Jones, I'm not the head of being called a liar. I don't like it. I said, I've got just one thing to say that I can prove every word I've got to say and you can't. I said, just come right along with me and go prove it right now. She had to turn turtle. She didn't go. I was going to take her in the pool right on the grocery store there now. He was the one that plowed the ground with the company team. The driver was the company man, too. They'd loaned them in to do their work.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Anyhow, I told her I said, I can prove what I've got to say and you can't. I was going to jump. I never felt like fighting before. I thought I was looking for a way to see men fight. I was over everything. But that day I was ready to clean her clock.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: She said, well, anyhow, she crawfished out of it somewhere. She had said that there's been a rush. She knew ours didn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What didn't rot? The markers?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: The markers. They would rot it off practically level with the ground there. They'd knock holes and things. She said, use for everything. Use of the survey. I said, we were planning on having that surveyed again. That's all you can do. I said, somebody hold a stake over the spot until they get it fixed up. I said, that's got to be resurveyed and you know it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What she was trying to do was take over the cemetery, the running of the cemetery plan and she didn't have anything to do with it. Well, I don't know whether she wanted to run it.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: She was trying to tell you how to do it. Yeah. I don't know whether she was or whether she wanted the money. But eventually she didn't get it. I don't know which it was. But she got a little money. She liked to keep it pretty well. But anyhow, she didn't want to go. I wanted to take her into it. So she didn't want to go. Well, anyhow, I don't remember how we got out of cement. I don't remember how that kid come out. What I said.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did she give you back your money?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: No, she didn't. Not then. And it went on and on and on. Well, we just dropped her after that. After she had that. And we just dropped her. Said, well, I guess you know, you isn't trying to do anything with her. We'll go ahead and have something else and make some more money. And then after, I guess it was a year or two, and she'd had this money all the time. I guess she finally got those ice cream paid for. And all these things that she said they owned. But we solicited the town for that money. They could have sued us for damages. We solicited the whole town for the cakes and pies. And the notes and things for that big dinner down there. And the whole town was, if they wanted to, they could have got after us for soliciting those eats and then not getting the thing.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Everybody was mad about it. But now she didn't kick through with anything then. And a year or two later, I got so, well, I didn't speak to her for a long time. And finally I thought, that's hurting me too. I thought that you can just carry an old grudge around in your gizzard until it'll eat you up. If you carry it long enough, it'll bring you up and you'll suffer for it yourself. I know I suffered everything for that because I talked with the ladies and all the big baby. Well, they didn't think much about me, but I felt kind of conscious over it. They didn't blame you, but you felt bad about it. No, I felt bad. I think I was at the head of it and wasn't getting anything done. And so, I was down at the store one day. Well, I prayed about it. I thought, well, we were told to pray for your enemies instead of just splicing it on. And you're supposed to do a lot of these things in a more Christian -like way. And I thought, well, I've got to take a different attitude towards this because it's just hurting me all over. And so, I did. I began to think things over. And I thought, well, I'll speak to her on the streets as they go along. And I could do it with her in particular, but I'll speak to her as a friend. And so, I'm a lot of a business. That's what I did from there on. Well, I don't know that she's spoken up, but I spoke to her. And one day there in the grocery store, it was a year or two later, and she came to me and she was just a nice little pie. You never saw. She just, the butter wouldn't melt in her mouth. She was so nice.
